mapping.a_element: random process of adding edges to an initially empty graph
mapping.b_element: graph evolution process adding edges to an initially empty graph

mapping.a_element: choice among K randomly presented edges at each round
mapping.b_element: selection of one edge from a small set of potential edges at each step

mapping.a_element: threshold for Hamiltonicity (appearance of a Hamilton cycle) depending on K
mapping.b_element: discontinuous percolation transition (emergence of a giant component)

mapping.a_element: regimes of behavior (subcritical, critical, supercritical) based on K's relation to log n
mapping.b_element: scaling behaviors and phase transition characteristics differing from classic models

mapping.a_element: analysis via combinatorial and probabilistic methods to determine thresholds
mapping.b_element: analysis via system of differential equations to describe evolution

brief_justification: While both papers study edge-addition processes in random graphs with choice, Paper A focuses on the combinatorial threshold for Hamiltonicity (a specific cycle structure) using probabilistic methods, whereas Paper B analyzes the emergence of a giant component via differential equations describing local cluster aggregation. The core mechanisms—Hamilton cycle formation vs. percolation transition—are fundamentally different, despite superficial similarities in edge-selection dynamics.
